speaker: interviewer
text: --in '96. Shmuel, you-- you are now in the Urals, in Magnitogorsk.

speaker: interviewee
text: I would like just to go back now, in the little story I just mentioned. This is before Magnitogorsk. This is was while I was still in Bobruisk. I was in Bobruisk.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I had people starting sending parcels to their relatives in Poland, where people were under the Nazis. And I organized a parcel. I even remember knotting the nails together. I can visualize it. I remember it. Send it away to Poland. And a few months later, I'm getting a letter from my brother-- my father didn't acknowledge it-- from my brother. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] [SOB] I have to stop now for a minute. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] --thanking me for the parcel, and reminding me that he knows where I am. And I should remember that there is a God. There is a God. Because I knew that I am not religious minded anymore, living among these Bolsheviks. So he was concerned at that time, while the Nazis were around. I shouldn't lose my belief.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was a different mindset?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was always different to my brother. Two brothers, two different worlds. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He-- he had an easy death, because I'm sure he, with his belief, went to death, as accept it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was--

speaker: interviewee
text: God's will.

speaker: interviewer
text: --God's will.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Now we can go back there.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you think about these times, these travels through Russia, do have any pictures in your mind? Can you see things and events?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only certain junctions.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you see?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well I can see when I came to Magnitogorsk, and [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] being sent away. They didn't let us stay there. We didn't have much trust in the people who were running. And I was sent away to a place called, and don't ask me to spell it, please, Verkhneural'sk. This is a Russian word, a place, where there's no train. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I think there were gold mines there, far away. This was the purpose of this [? process. ?] All these people were running there.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was the purpose of the Russian bureaucracy?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. [SIGHS] It was a very short period. Because I was with somebody else. And after a few days, I got up in the morning, and that fellow disappeared. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I said to myself, now is coming winter, because it became late. And in Ural, it's very, very cold. And I said to myself, in a time of war, go where it's warm. Then you'll have less worry. Because cold, and hunger, is a bad combination. So I set out back to Magnitogorsk, where I didn't have a very pleasant reception from the authorities.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get back?

speaker: interviewee
text: Running.

speaker: interviewer
text: Again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Just now-- just now you asked about certain events. I was running, and just no-- I didn't know where to. I just looked by lights. You know, just play stranger, strange, far away. And I was thirsty. And I heard-- I heard-- the noise of-- what do you call that creature in the mud, that what you have got in the larger size in Queensland?

speaker: interviewer
text: The frog? Toad?

speaker: interviewee
text: Frog. Frog. Not a toad. The frogs. So I went straight to the water. It was mud. Stretched myself out, licked the mud. I asked this outstanding, this is the kind of situation you can remember, of course, because it's--

speaker: interviewer
text: You were so--

speaker: interviewee
text: --so thirsty, and nowhere to go, and nowhere to drink, and nowhere-- you don't know where. I remember I reached Magnitogorsk. And I didn't get a very sympathetic reception from the authorities.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now tell me about the authorities. Every movement was-- had to be approved by them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not necessarily. The confusion was so great, that they tried to control. But they couldn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: There were just too many.

speaker: interviewee
text: Too many. Too many. How I succeeded, I don't remember. I took off by train and went to Middle Asia, a large city called Tashkent. I reached Tashkent. I don't know how. But a large city, this is a million-- a city of millions now. It's a well-known city now. And there, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I was in a park with thousands and thousands of people, lying in-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in the park and spending the time, looking for bread, sleeping there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what time of year are we talking about?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was end of summer, '41.

speaker: interviewer
text: So by the end of summer, '41, you had got yourself by hook or by crook--

speaker: interviewee
text: By hook or by crook

speaker: interviewer
text: --to Tashkent.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Now this, I was lucky enough. While I was lying there in-- in Tashkent in a park, stretched out, with my whole belongings with me--

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your belongings?

speaker: interviewee
text: A shirt perhaps. I don't know. Nothing. I didn't have anything. The most cherished belong was a piece of bread, a slice of bread. At that time, it already was a great problem. Suddenly came an elderly woman, she was from Warsaw, a Jewish woman who was-- her face, I remember. Her face I definitely remember-- an angelic face. And she was looking if she has got any relatives that came from Warsaw. Because there was a flood of people. And she just approached me and asked my conditions. And when I explained to her the situation, she said, told me, look. I live here.

speaker: interviewer
text: She had been living in Tashkent for a long time?

speaker: interviewee
text: For many years, for many years, since the First World War, when she got married an Armenian Catholic. I will give you the address. I leave the door open. And at nighttime, go in. And there was a place there where they keep the wood, you know, for heating, and cooking. You can go in there and sleep there. And I will leave you a bit of food there as well.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was the arrangement she made with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was-- this was like saving. It saved me. That was a great thing. That is more than winning now the lottery instantly.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she really was responsible for keeping--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was there for a very long time-- a very long time. I don't know how long. Long months.

speaker: interviewer
text: Many months.

speaker: interviewee
text: Many months.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you slept while in her wood pile?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she left you a bit of food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, every evening I came with the food there as well.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now her husband never knew you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Never knew.

speaker: interviewer
text: He would have objected enormously to this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I'm sure of that. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did she do it?

speaker: interviewee
text: They are good people. She was a good person.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Funny thing. I don't remember her, but I remember his.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: He is an Armenian called Sarkis. This is an Armenian name, Sarkis [? Kalpavich. ?] His father's name was [? Karp. ?] Well, his name-- this is-- there's no justification for it. But I don't remember. Her face, I remember quite well. His name, I remember quite well.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she was your saving angel.

speaker: interviewee
text: My saving angel, yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had it not been for this woman, what would have happened to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, what happened then is that after a while, the authorities started making-- I don't how. There's no word for it even in the English language. When they close up everything-- [NON-ENGLISH] it's called in Russian-- when they close a whole area and started checking papers, or lack of papers. There's a word for it in Russian.

speaker: interviewer
text: So basically, what they did was they cordoned off an area.

speaker: interviewee
text: Cordoned off the area, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were stuck?

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were stuck. And then they send you away where they wanted.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that they found out who was who, where they were from?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who was who, and who hadn't the papers necessary.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now did you have any papers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this is another story. Somewhere on the way, somebody stole my passport.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your Polish passport?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not Polish, by that time-- Russian passport.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your Russian passport now.

speaker: interviewee
text: My Russian passport, yes. They didn't have a Polish passport then. [INAUDIBLE] passport was-- I didn't have a Polish passport in my life. I was young. I had a Russian passport, but it was stolen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you get this passport? Which city?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Bobruisk.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was issued in Bobruisk.

speaker: interviewee
text: Issued in Bobruisk, yes. This was-- this was a system, a Soviet system, that was a passport with a paragraph. Which means, have you committed any sin? Are you a type that is not completely to be trusted? And I remember the paragraph. My paragraph was paragraph 38.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did that say?

speaker: interviewee
text: That means that I'm coming from Poland, something-- something not kosher. I'm not to be trusted.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ah. So you were identified in this passport as being--

speaker: interviewee
text: As having-- that's not kosher.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not the right background?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sorry, sorry an expression. If you don't follow it, I-- yes. Not--

speaker: interviewer
text: --totally to be trusted.

speaker: interviewee
text: --totally to be trusted, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this-- this passport was stolen?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was stolen. Well, all I had left in my pocket was a member of the union. This was, by the way, not voluntary work, the union. When you work in Russia, you are a member of the union. And that was on the document also, left in my pocket. But this-- this document I came to Tashkent. By the way, when I was in Tashkent and living in that place in--

speaker: interviewer
text: In the wood pile?

speaker: interviewee
text: --in the wood pile, I had to go out in the morning before the gentleman got up, not to see me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was very dangerous. Because I got up in the morning very early. And the police is everywhere. Oftentimes I [INAUDIBLE] had problems. Once I had a problem. There was such a cordoned-off place. And I was taken to a place where there's all kind of old things. And the police were waiting to sort out people, to send them out. What they did, they sent them out to the villages, called [NON-ENGLISH]. it's called in Russian term-- collective village. And the trouble was, it wouldn't be bad, because you had what to eat. But they send you mostly in cotton villages, where they make cotton. So only thing you could eat was cotton.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was cotton and no food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Cotton and no food, which was a half death sentence.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were trying not to be caught up by the Russian authority?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I was trapped. I was trapped to be sent out. The interesting thing is when-- when they caught you, they didn't ask you the name, because most people didn't have any names. It was all kinds of vagrants, you know. So they just give you a number, number 21, 22. Anyway, I observed some-- a local fellow. And I had a piece of bread. I told him, I will give you the piece of bread, and let me in among these junk, to hide. So after they called, and they call-- after a while it was finished. And they opened the gates again. I left. And this is how I saved myself at that time, from a cotton expedition.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your aim was to remain in Tashkent, and to not to be sent-- not to be-- you didn't want to be sent by the authorities anywhere?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, because there was nothing to eat.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you had no job. And you had no--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no job. That was for a while-- quite a while. I don't remember how it came to it. But anyway, after awhile, I shifted to a place outside Tashkent called [? Lunachowsk ?]. And there I stayed till the end of the war. I worked there, not work. I had a working place. I was a-- [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] Let me think about it. I remember it in Russian. [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] The nearest, it would be a guard. You know, a guard, a factory. At night. And actually, I slept inside.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were a watchman, or a guard?

speaker: interviewee
text: A watchman. Oh, thank you. That was the right word. That's the right word, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you looked after a factory.

speaker: interviewee
text: After a factory, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So somewhere the Russian authorities caught up with you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I caught up with them. They caught up with me.

words.text: them. They caught up with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or you caught up with them.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was a very convenient place of work. Because I was not well. There was very little to eat at that time, very little.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now tell me about this food situation.

speaker: interviewee
text: Food situation was horrible, horrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: So people were starving?

speaker: interviewee
text: Starving.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're talking now people in-- in Central Asia, Russia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sick people, local people, any people. It was terrible. You have to realize the men who fell at war.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what? All food that existed was going to the war effort?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's correct. The collective settlements were empty for-- for men folk. The situation was terrible. We got 400 gram of bread a day, and that was all. Nothing more than that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the bread was made of flour and lots of water. Why lots of water? Because the people who worked at the factory, in the bakery, stole the flour, and add more water. So actually the bread was like sticked-- like piece of dough bread. This we had 400 gram, which was a slice. And this was for a couple of years, the only source of food. That was all you could get?

speaker: interviewee
text: All you could get. You got a card, yes, and you had coupons. And the coupons gave you this food. That was all. Nothing more.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was the official food?

speaker: interviewee
text: The official. Non-official, different story.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened non-officially?

speaker: interviewee
text: Non-officially, you could steal, if you can. I couldn't. So you could deal. I wasn't a great salesman.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you try?

speaker: interviewee
text: I tried, but it was very, very hard. It was-- these days were terrible. Luckily, I was so sick, that I was taken to hospital. The sickness was called [INAUDIBLE]. I think I told you before. There's a sickness. People who haven't got the-- don't eat vegetables, sick, skin is cracking up. This a process that one dies from that, little by little. But I was taken in time.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your-- you condition was so bad.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I was taken to hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were starving along with-- With the [INTERPOSING VOICES] --everyone else?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you became so sick.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. That I was taken to a hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: When would this have been?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say end of '42. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Yes. '42. I'll tell you why. Because in this place, when Stalingrad was-- the famous Battle of Stalingrad, when the-- the Nazis got their first defeat. I was in this place.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the hospital?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I was outside of the hospital then.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was before?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Stalingrad fell.

speaker: interviewee
text: Later.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were getting sicker, and sicker, and sicker. And it was after the fall of Stalingrad, after the victory, the Russian victory and German defeat of Stalingrad?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I would like, by the way, now, to add something which I just remembered. For one period, the source of my income was, I used to come to the post office and write letters for illiterate Russians who wanted to write to their sons on the front. There were women who couldn't write Russian. And I used to sit, write for them the letters. And I used to get an onion, a couple of potatoes, a piece of bread, you know. [INAUDIBLE] And that was--

speaker: interviewer
text: That was how you managed to do a little bit?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. Additional food. And then I came to hospital. And in the hospital, I had [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the bare minimum. They didn't feed like here in a hospital. You got a soup that was made of water and salt and some leaves, and the bread ration, which was the same. That was quite a long time.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in hospital?

speaker: interviewee
text: Six months.

speaker: interviewer
text: Six months?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Approximately six months. And when I came out of hospital, I found a new vocation. I started polishing shoes, boots.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were your clients?

speaker: interviewee
text: My clients were the local people, the Asians who were living on-- on the collective villages, you know. And these were mostly communist party members. They used to come for gatherings. Yes. And it was a dusty place. They used to come.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you have a shoe polishing outfit?

speaker: interviewee
text: A shoe polishing outfit. And they used to call me professor, because I had glasses. That was a sign.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were the shoe polishing-- the shoe polishing professor.

speaker: interviewee
text: The shoe polishing professor. And this was already-- this time started already to get easier. Because with-- with the time-- as a matter of fact, by the beginning of 1944, the situation got easier with food.

speaker: interviewer
text: So as-- as the Russian victories increased--

speaker: interviewee
text: Increased, so the food situation was-- why, I don't know. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: But there was an appreciable improvement in the food. And after your-- you obviously got better in hospital, eventually.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You went-- after your six months, you were more or less healthy?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Weak, but healthy. Yes. I came out and started working in this shoe business, which was.

speaker: interviewer
text: You never went back to being the watchman in the factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This was additional. The watchman was at night, and this was the day.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ah. So the shoes were your day job.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And night job, this I-- this I kept till I-- till I left Russia on the way back to Poland, by the end of the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you actually lived in the factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I slept there at night.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which you had a place to sleep somewhere.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. A place on the floor.

speaker: interviewer
text: Somewhere in the factory.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So things improved a little. You-- you had two jobs.

speaker: interviewee
text: Two jobs, and I don't remember being hungry anymore. Because the bad times during the worst time of the war, the constant nagging thought was, where do I get something to eat?

speaker: interviewer
text: Those were those early days in Tashkent?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, the war. How much knowledge did you have of what was happening? You must have got Russian propaganda?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well at that time I wasn't interested in not listening to their propaganda. Their propaganda was my hope for surviving. I used to come in the middle of the day in the library. And I used to take the newspapers. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was the Tashkent main library?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, not at Tashkent's, [? Lunachowsk's. ?] This is--

speaker: interviewer
text: [? Lunachowsk. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: And I used to read. Besides, in the street were loudspeakers. Yes? And they used to give the news, what happens, what the situation is in the world. So I had an idea. I remember by the way, the first time-- that was the end of the war, of course. That was in August '45. I was sitting here reading the paper, that Americans dropped a bomb that was 20,000 ton, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the force of 20,000 ton of TNT. And I said to myself, this must be a mistake. This is impossible. A bomb is 5,000. That was the first atom bomb.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you read about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: In [? Lunachowsk. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What information did you get from the loudspeakers and from the newspapers in the library?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh the newspapers was the newspapers. The Russian newspapers all [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] propaganda, propaganda, and propaganda. And if you got a little bit of brain, you have just to understand and decide for yourself what is true and what is not true. But mostly, the situation, the front you could visualize.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's say if when the Germans were going forward and conquering this city, and that city, you used to say, after a long and bitter struggle, the city was evacuated. This was the language. So I knew what had happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: And let's say when Stalingrad, which was the turning point, Stalingrad, that was, let's see, January '43 was the turning point in the Second World War, where the Germans-- I at that time, made up my mind, I, myself-- the Germans have lost the war. It was the first time that the whole army was surrounded, German army defeated. And as a matter of fact, from that time, the Russians started going forward all the time. That was the last piece of land that the Germans reached, was this. From then on--

speaker: interviewer
text: It was back the other way.

speaker: interviewee
text: Back the other way.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you believe all the time that you would survive? Or only from this point on?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I somehow had a hope I would survive. I don't-- I couldn't visualize otherwise. Otherwise I wouldn't have survived, perhaps.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had a strong belief that-- that you would make it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the news started to filter through. You knew that things were--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --going the way of the Russians?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. From 1943, they-- they said they have taken 110, 120,000 Germans-- [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] how do you say? And also I remember the Russian word, [INAUDIBLE]. They have taken them in [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] captivity. Would that be correct word? With the field marshal. This was the beginning of the end of the Germans, of the Nazi Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever in the news that was provided by the Russians, hear anything about the Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: As far as I remember, the uprising in Warsaw ghetto, I remember in '43.

speaker: interviewer
text: You heard about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was a Jewish uprising? That wasn't a workers' uprising? That was a--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They usually-- they usually tried to hide the Jewish part of it, for obvious reason. The Nazis presented their struggle-- their war as a war against Jews. The Russians, the communists, presented their struggle as a struggle for freedom, for democracy. Now if you say the word, Jews, you-- you bring in your work, the Jewish term again. So they very seldom used the word Jews. Very seldom.

speaker: interviewer
text: But with the Warsaw uprising they did.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was, yes, that was as an event.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now you stayed in [? Lunachowsk ?] until when?

speaker: interviewee
text: Until September or October '45.

speaker: interviewer
text: And at that stage, you knew that--

speaker: interviewee
text: At that stage, I had already-- people started disappearing. What means disappearing? People, together with us, the Polish colony, started little, one by one, disappearing. So we got to know that they are leaving. What I did is I looked up again this woman.

speaker: interviewer
text: The lady who had helped you.

speaker: interviewee
text: The lady. And I asked her if she can get for me documents, I should be able to get out and go back to Poland. And this is how it happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: She did that for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you give her any money?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she managed to--

speaker: interviewee
text: For me and a group of three people, four people who left.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ah. So there were-- there was a group of you. She got several sets of documents.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Documents for each one of us. Four people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Four lots of documents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Four lots of documents, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: These were forgeries.

speaker: interviewee
text: Forgeries, yes. Obviously forgeries.

speaker: interviewer
text: Saying what?

speaker: interviewee
text: That we are officers going back from-- from a special job. This was a highly risky-- looking back, you see, I took a great risk.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: What happened? [INAUDIBLE] What happened? I went from there to Moscow, via Kiev. No. To Moscow. First to Moscow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have trouble with the documents?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a little story in itself. I went to Moscow, from Moscow to Kiev. And from Kiev, I was in Moscow a few days. And from Kiev, I went by train to-- these documents had the destination, Lviv. Lviv was the Soviet side. Krakow was already not the Soviet side. On the train, while we were travelling, the four of us, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] there was such an institution, Soviet Russia's train police, police who just bother about the trains. They cordoned off the train and started looking for documents. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And these four people of us, each one of us looked so typical Jewish, non-military type, that we had decided to divide our group of four, two and two. And so not to-- the attention shouldn't be so obvious. Anyway, so the police came up, a police officer. Secret police, [NON-ENGLISH] it was called at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: NKVD.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]. Yes, this is abbreviation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes and what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: A fellow took the documents, started looking. And I nearly lost my soul. Because I knew it could be end of my life. He looked at me, and he said in Russian-- he said in Russian-- you're going to Lviv, and Lviv to Krakow? Which means the other side. You want to over the border. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I said, yes. He shook my hands, and just gave a sign that he's Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was a Jewish NKVD officer?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were very lucky.

speaker: interviewee
text: Otherwise, after train, to jail, and the end. So you had to be lucky quite a few times. Not once.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's stop there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.
